description: I was the only observer. I camped at this location on the night of July 15-16th. I heard crossbills on the evening of 7/15, but only managed to get poor cell phone recordings. I assumed the birds were the expected Type 5 "Lodgepole Pine" RECR because of the predominance of Lodgepole Pine across the landscape and because the birds didn't sound like the Type 2 "Ponderosa Pine" crossbills that I have the most experience with. When I woke on the morning of July 16th, I could hear crossbills in the nearby forests. I immediately got my Tascam DR-100mkiii recorder and Senneiser ME66/K6 shotgun microphone set up to record the crossbills hoping to obtain good recordings of Type 5 RECR. I spent a solid 25 minutes with a small group of crossbills at close range, at one point <5m. The group consisted of at least one adult male, an adult female, and a juvenile. Other crossbills were heard nearby, but these three were the target of my recordings. I recorded several tracks of the birds, including some that were 5+ minutes long. The birds were foraging in the Lodgepole Pines. Most pines in the area had two generations of cones, some old, brown cones and fresh, green cones. I was able to capture a variety of calls including typical flight calls, alarm "toops" calls, what I think may be juvenile begging calls ("chew-wheer"), and various feeding chatter. The flight calls, in particular, are diagnostic among crossbills and provide the most compelling evidence of the specific ID. The male sang some half-hearted songs. The plumage was typical for a Red/Cassia Crossbill. The male was a dirty red color, and the female was streaky but with some greenish hues. The juvenile was more heavily streaked. All had "cross bills". Although I recorded these birds in July 2021, I did not get around to downloading the files and analyzing them until November 2021. It was at this point that I realized that these were Cassia Crossbills, and not the expected Type 5 Red Crossbill. The details of the spectrograms are unoquivocal. The recordings have been reviewed by Tim Spahr and Matt Young and they confirm that these are Cassia Crossbills (formerly Type 9 Red Crossbill).
photographer: Photographer: Christian Nunes, Christian Nunes, Peter Gent, Peter Gent, Peter Gent, Peter Gent
